text: Concerning baptism, baptize thus: having first said all these things, baptize into the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit in living water.
Wherever the bishop appears, there let the multitude be; even as wherever Jesus Christ is, there is the catholic Church.
It is within the power of all, therefore, in every Church, who may wish to see the truth, to contemplate clearly the tradition of the apostles manifested throughout the whole world.

The Apostles received the gospel for us from the Lord Jesus Christ; Jesus Christ was sent from God. Christ therefore is from God and the Apostles are from Christ. Both came in due order by the will of God… They appointed their first-fruits, having first proved them by the Spirit, to be bishops and deacons of those who should believe.

Our apostles also knew, through our Lord Jesus Christ, that there would be strife on account of the office of the episcopate. For this reason, therefore, since they had obtained a perfect foreknowledge of this, they appointed those already mentioned, and afterwards gave instructions, that when these should fall asleep, other approved men should succeed them in their ministry.
I am God's wheat, and I am ground by the teeth of wild beasts that I may be found pure bread of Christ.
Breaking one bread, which is the medicine of immortality, and the antidote that we should not die, but live for ever in Jesus Christ.

They abstain from the Eucharist and from prayer, because they confess not the Eucharist to be the flesh of our Saviour Jesus Christ, which suffered for our sins, and which the Father, of His goodness, raised up again.

Jesus Christ, who was of the race of David, who was the Son of Mary, who was truly born and ate and drank, was truly persecuted under Pontius Pilate, was truly crucified and died in the sight of those in heaven and on earth and under the earth; who was also truly raised from the dead, His Father having raised Him.
notes: Anti-docetist emphasis on the true bodily reality of Christ's life, death, and resurrection

text: For whosoever does not confess that Jesus Christ has come in the flesh, is antichrist; and whosoever does not confess the testimony of the Cross, is of the devil; and whosoever perverts the oracles of the Lord to his own lusts, and says that there is neither a resurrection nor a judgment, he is the firstborn of Satan.

For not as common bread and common drink do we receive these; but in like manner as Jesus Christ our Saviour, having been made flesh by the Word of God, had both flesh and blood for our salvation, so likewise have we been taught that the food which is blessed by the prayer of His word, and from which our blood and flesh by transmutation are nourished, is the flesh and blood of that Jesus who was made flesh.

As many as are persuaded and believe that what we teach and say is true, and undertake to be able to live accordingly, are instructed to pray and to entreat God with fasting… Then they are brought by us where there is water, and are regenerated in the same manner in which we were ourselves regenerated. For, in the name of God, the Father and Lord of the universe, and of our Saviour Jesus Christ, and of the Holy Spirit, they then receive the washing with water.

And on the day called Sunday, all who live in cities or in the country gather together to one place, and the memoirs of the apostles or the writings of the prophets are read… Then we all rise together and pray, and, as we before said, when our prayer is ended, bread and wine and water are brought, and the president in like manner offers prayers and thanksgivings, according to his ability, and the people assent, saying Amen; and there is a distribution to each, and a participation of that over which thanks have been given.
notes: Earliest detailed description of Sunday Eucharistic liturgy

text: Since, however, it would be very tedious, in such a volume as this, to reckon up the successions of all the Churches, we do put to confusion all those who… assemble in unauthorized meetings; we do this by indicating that tradition derived from the apostles, of the very great, the very ancient, and universally known Church founded and organized at Rome by the two most glorious apostles, Peter and Paul.
The glory of God is a living man; and the life of man consists in beholding God.
notes: Often rendered 'Gloria Dei vivens homo'; key statement of Irenaeus's anthropology and soteriology

text: The Word of God, our Lord Jesus Christ, who did, through His transcendent love, become what we are, that He might bring us to be even what He Himself is.

In the times of the kingdom, the righteous man who is upon the earth shall then forget to die. For there shall be no more death, neither sorrow, neither shall there be any more pain… and it is just that in the very creation in which they toiled or were afflicted… in the very same, the just shall reign.

All are of One, by unity of substance; while the mystery of the dispensation is still guarded, which distributes the Unity into a Trinity, placing in their order the three Persons — the Father, the Son, and the Holy Ghost: three, however, not in condition, but in degree; not in substance, but in form; not in power, but in aspect.
notes: First use of Latin Trinitas (Trinity) in a fully theological sense; introduces tres personae, una substantia

text: The rule of faith, indeed, is altogether one, alone immoveable and irreformable; the rule, to wit, of believing in one only God omnipotent, the Creator of the universe, and His Son Jesus Christ, born of the Virgin Mary, crucified under Pontius Pilate, raised again the third day from the dead, received in the heavens, sitting now at the right of the Father, destined to come to judge the quick and the dead through the resurrection of the flesh as well as of the spirit.
Happy is our sacrament of water, in that, by washing away the sins of our early blindness, we are set free and admitted into eternal life!

Now about the Eucharist: This is how to give thanks: First in connection with the cup: 'We thank you, our Father, for the holy vine of David your servant, which you have revealed through Jesus your servant. To you be glory forever.' Then in connection with the piece broken off: 'We thank you, our Father, for the life and knowledge which you have revealed through Jesus your servant. To you be glory forever.'
He can no longer have God for his Father, who has not the Church for his mother; if any one could escape who abandoned the ark of Noah, then he also may escape who shall be without the Church.
work_reference: On the Unity of the Church 6

text: The days will come in which vines shall grow, each having ten thousand branches, and in each branch ten thousand twigs, and in each true twig ten thousand shoots, and in each one of the shoots ten thousand clusters, and on every one of the clusters ten thousand grapes, and every grape when pressed will give five-and-twenty metretes of wine.
notes: Papias quotes this as a saying of the Lord preserved by elders who knew the apostles; represents chiliastic hope for the coming kingdom

text: But I and others, who are right-minded Christians on all points, are assured that there will be a resurrection of the dead, and a thousand years in Jerusalem, which will then be built, adorned, and enlarged, as the prophets Ezekiel and Isaiah and others declare.
The Word of God became man, that you may learn from man how man may become God.
There is no time when [the Son] was not. For God the Father never existed when the Word was not… He is the eternal and everlasting Word.
notes: Origen asserts co-eternity of the Son with the Father; though Origen's subordinationism is also present in De Principiis, this passage strongly affirms the eternal generation

text: When the person being baptized goes down into the water, he who baptizes him, putting his hand on him, shall say: 'Do you believe in God, the Father Almighty?' And he who is being baptized shall say: 'I believe.' Then holding his hand on his head, he shall baptize him once.
notes: One of the oldest surviving accounts of the baptismal rite; triple interrogatory immersion form

text: This is the one who made heaven and earth, and fashioned humankind in the beginning, who was proclaimed through the law and the prophets, who was enfleshed in a virgin, who was hanged on a tree, who was buried in earth, who was raised from the dead and went up to the heights of heaven… He is the Christ. He is the King forever and ever. Amen.
notes: Melito's climactic confession of Christ as cosmic King and fulfillment of the Passover

text: The oftener we are mown down by you, the more in number we grow; the blood of Christians is seed.
notes: The origin of the famous expression "the blood of martyrs is the seed of the Church"

text: We define that there are two, the Father and the Son, and with the Holy Spirit three, according to the principle of the divine economy which makes the number of the Trinity.
notes: One of Tertullian's clearest statements of three-person Trinitarian theology

text: God made all things from nothing. For this is the first point: that nothing was co-eternal with God, nothing co-unoriginate, nothing co-equal with God, lest, if something had been equally unborn and unmade and co-eternal with God, it should be pronounced God.
notes: Tertullian's clearest direct statement of creatio ex nihilo

text: We think that the goodness of God, through his Christ, will restore his entire creation to one end, even his enemies being conquered and subdued.
notes: Origen's foundational statement of universal restoration (apocatastasis)

text: The Scriptures were composed through the Spirit of God, and they have not only that meaning which is obvious, but also another which is hidden from the majority of readers. For the contents of Scripture are the outward forms of certain mysteries and images of divine things.
notes: Origen's foundational statement of allegorical/threefold biblical interpretation

text: You cannot have God for your Father if you have not the Church for your mother. If anyone could escape outside Noah's ark, then anyone outside the Church may also escape.
notes: One of the most influential ecclesiological statements in patristic literature

text: The episcopate is one, each part of which is held by each one for the whole. The Church also is one, which is spread abroad far and wide into a multitude by an increase of fruitfulness.
notes: Cyprian's foundational statement of episcopal collegiality and church unity

text: Each one must do penance for his sin, and by the remedies of repentance make satisfaction to God: that the sinner must be humble in his grief, sad in his mourning, prostrate in his fasting… that he must implore God with tears, that he must implore his brethren with tears.
notes: Cyprian's articulation of the graduated penitential process for the lapsed

text: Lord God Almighty, Father of thy well-beloved and blessed Son Jesus Christ, through whom we have received the knowledge of thee… I bless thee, because thou hast granted me this day and this hour, that I might take a portion amongst the number of the martyrs.
notes: Polycarp's prayer immediately before execution; one of the oldest verbatim martyr prayers preserved

text: The Lord, coming to the lost sheep and making so great a recapitulation of the economy of salvation, also recapitulated in himself the long line of human beings, and furnished us, in a brief and comprehensive manner, with salvation, so that what we had lost in Adam, namely, to be according to the image and likeness of God, that we might recover in Christ Jesus.
notes: Irenaeus's clearest statement of the recapitulation of Adam in Christ

text: He is himself uncreated, both without beginning and without end, and lacking nothing, and self-sufficient, and giving existence to all other things; but the things which have been made by him have received a beginning.
notes: Irenaeus's direct contrast of God's self-sufficiency with the creature's derived existence

text: If I say that the Father suffered and died, I make him passible and mortal. But it was not the Father who became flesh and suffered, but the Son. The Father is he who commands, and the Son is he who obeys.
notes: Hippolytus's direct refutation of Noetus: the Father cannot suffer, only the Son

text: In like manner also the three days which were before the luminaries are types of the Trinity: of God, and his Word, and his Wisdom.
notes: First known use of the Greek word Trias (Trinity) in Christian theology

text: For not as common bread and common drink do we receive these; but in like manner as Jesus Christ our Savior, having been made flesh by the Word of God, had both flesh and blood for our salvation, so likewise have we been taught that the food which is blessed by the prayer of his word, and from which our blood and flesh by transmutation are nourished, is the flesh and blood of that Jesus who was made flesh.
notes: One of the most explicit 2nd-century statements of eucharistic real presence

text: God is not only the founder of this religion, but also its vindicator. For if anyone on earth has so great power as to be able to give laws to many nations, how much greater power has he who formed all nations, who reigns over all, who controls all things by his divine providence!
notes: Lactantius's apology for providential governance underpinning the persecution narratives

text: Neither, therefore, may we divide into three gods the wonderful and divine unity; nor may we depreciate the dignity and exceeding majesty of the Lord by the name of a mere creature; but we must believe in God the Father Almighty, and in Christ Jesus his Son, and in the Holy Spirit, and that the Word is united to the God of all.
work_reference: Letter to Dionysius of Rome (in Athanasius, De Sent. Dionys. 13)
notes: Dionysius of Alexandria correcting his earlier inadvertent tritheism; key pre-Nicene Trinitarian document

text: The days will come in which vines shall grow, each having ten thousand branches, and in each branch ten thousand twigs, and in each twig ten thousand shoots, and in every one of the shoots ten thousand clusters, and in every cluster ten thousand grapes, and every grape when pressed will give five-and-twenty metretes of wine.
notes: Papias's vivid millenarian prophecy, which he attributed to the Lord himself and which deeply influenced Irenaeus

text: Our Apostles also knew, through our Lord Jesus Christ, that there would be strife over the name of the bishop's office. For this reason, having received perfect foreknowledge, they appointed those who have been mentioned, and afterwards added the further provision that, if they should fall asleep, other approved men should succeed to their ministry.
notes: The locus classicus for apostolic succession theology in early Christianity

text: Let no man do anything connected with the Church without the bishop. Let that be deemed a proper Eucharist, which is either under the bishop, or under one to whom he shall have entrusted it.
notes: Ignatius's clearest statement of the monarchical bishop as the centre of valid sacramental life

text: Victor, who presided over the church at Rome, immediately attempted to cut off from the common unity the parishes of all Asia, with the churches that agreed with them, as heterodox; and he wrote letters and declared all the brethren there wholly excommunicate.
notes: Eusebius's account of Victor I's attempted excommunication of Asia — the first recorded Roman claim of universal disciplinary authority

text: It is impossible for man ever to arrive at the resurrection unless he has first passed through this earthly life; and for that reason the body rises in order that we may receive the recompense of our deeds done in the body.
notes: Methodius's insistence on bodily resurrection against Origen's spiritual resurrection doctrine

text: Among the disputed books, which are nevertheless recognized by many, are extant the Epistle of James, as it is called, and that of Jude, and the second epistle of Peter, and those that are called the second and third of John.
notes: Eusebius's four-category canon classification is the earliest systematic attempt at a NT canon list

text: Mark, having become the interpreter of Peter, wrote down accurately whatsoever he remembered. It was not, however, in exact order that he related the sayings or deeds of Christ. For he neither heard the Lord nor accompanied him.
notes: Oldest external testimony about the composition of the Gospel of Mark; links it to Peter's preaching

text: For he was made man that we might be made God; and he manifested himself by a body that we might receive the idea of the unseen Father; and he endured the insolence of men that we might inherit immortality.
notes: Athanasius's classic statement of theosis through the Incarnation

text: The Word of God, incorporeal and incorruptible and immaterial, came into our world. Not that he had been remote from it before; he was never without his Father's presence. But he put on a human body for the first time, that, so appearing, he might teach men to know him as God.
notes: Athanasius's statement of the Logos assuming humanity without ceasing to be divine

text: The flesh is the hinge of salvation.
notes: Tertullian's famous axiom caro salutis cardo: refutes Docetic denial of Christ's real flesh

text: Every one who is desirous of constructing out of the holy scriptures one connected body of doctrine must use points like these as his elementary and foundation principles.
notes: Origen's programmatic statement on Scripture as the source of systematic Christian doctrine

text: To this Church, on account of its superior origin, all churches must agree — that is, all the faithful everywhere — in which church ever the tradition from the Apostles has been preserved by those who are everywhere.
notes: Irenaeus's foundational statement on Roman succession as the criterion of apostolic tradition

text: It is not possible that the Gospels can be either more or fewer in number than they are. For, since there are four zones of the world in which we live, and four principal winds, while the Church is scattered throughout all the world, and the pillar and ground of the Church is the Gospel and the spirit of life; it is fitting that she should have four pillars.
notes: Irenaeus's famous fourfold Gospel argument; first explicit defense of exactly four canonical Gospels

text: The Gnostic is such that he is subject only to the affections that exist for the maintenance of the body, such as hunger, thirst, and the like. As for the Savior, it were ludicrous to suppose that he had any such wants. For he ate not for the sake of the body, but that the disciples might not entertain a wrong opinion respecting him.
notes: Clement's portrait of the true Gnostic as one who transcends passion through the Logos

text: He is God, but generated; he is God, but of the Father; he is God, but in the second place after the Father. He is also man — visible, capable of suffering, subject to the laws of mortality. He is also God — invisible, incapable of suffering, and therefore not subject to the laws of mortality.
notes: Novatian's clearest pre-Nicene statement of the Son as truly divine but generated and distinct

text: We expand the monad indivisibly into the Trinity and correspondingly gather the Trinity back together without reduction into the monad.
work_reference: Letter to Dionysius of Rome (Athanasius, De Sent. Dionys. 17)
notes: Dionysius of Alexandria's corrected Trinitarian formula after the Sabellian controversy

text: There is one God, the Creator and Governor of all things; who made the world with a purpose — not because he needed the labor, but that he might set in motion the virtues through which the world should be governed.
notes: Lactantius's foundational monotheistic statement underlying his creatio ex nihilo theology

text: No one is born for himself; we are born for duty. Soldier, you are given not only to your commander but to your country, and to the state. Neither does the Christian belong to himself; he is given to God.
notes: Tertullian's exhortation to imprisoned Christians awaiting martyrdom; framing martyrdom as duty

text: As long as the things foretold did not come to pass, there could be no belief in them; but when they should be fulfilled, then they would be acknowledged.
notes: Hippolytus on the self-authenticating nature of fulfilled prophecy as a canon of truth

text: Now, may the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, and the eternal High Priest himself, the Son of God Jesus Christ, build you up in faith and truth, and in all gentleness and in all avoidance of wrath and in forbearance and long suffering, and in patient endurance and in purity; and may he give to you a lot and portion among his saints, and to us with you.
notes: Polycarp's benediction connecting resurrection hope and saintly fellowship

text: Let us consider, beloved, how the Lord is continually revealing to us the future resurrection of which he made the Lord Jesus Christ the first-fruits by raising him from the dead.
notes: 1 Clement's argument for resurrection from natural analogies — grain, the phoenix

text: But let no one eat or drink of your Eucharist, unless they have been baptized into the name of the Lord; for concerning this also the Lord has said: 'Do not give what is holy to dogs.'
notes: The Didache's restriction of the Eucharist to the baptized; one of the earliest eucharistic regulations

text: Breaking one bread, which is the medicine of immortality, and the antidote that we should not die, but live for ever in Jesus Christ.
notes: Ignatius's celebrated description of the Eucharist as the medicine of immortality

text: For each man spoke well in proportion to the share he had of the spermatic word, seeing what was related to it. But they who contradict themselves on the more important points appear not to have possessed the heavenly wisdom and the knowledge which cannot be spoken against.
notes: Justin's logos spermatikos (seed-word) doctrine: the divine Logos scattered seeds of truth in all human philosophy

text: After the example of John's baptism, we are immersed in water; but in accordance with the power of God, the Father and the Son and the Holy Spirit, over us.
notes: Tertullian's description of the Trinitarian baptismal formula and its regenerating power

text: Whoever is separated from the Church is joined to an adulteress, is cut off from the promises of the Church; nor can he who forsakes the Church of Christ attain to the rewards of Christ. He is a stranger; he is profane; he is an enemy.
notes: Cyprian's famous extra ecclesiam nulla salus — no salvation outside the Church

text: God was not always Father; but there was a time when God was alone, and was not yet Father, but afterwards he became Father. The Son was not always; for, whereas all things were made out of nothing, and all existing creatures and works were made, so also the Word of God himself was made out of nothing.
work_reference: Arius, Thalia (preserved in Athanasius, De Synodis 15)
notes: The key Arian proposition: the Son has a beginning and was made from nothing; Athanasius quotes it as the purest expression of Arian theology

text: The bishops of the surrounding country and cities came together with great frequency to Antioch… the one who had committed an offense against the faith was Paul of Samosata, who entertained low and degraded views of Christ, contrary to the teaching of the Church, namely, that he was in his nature an ordinary man.
notes: Eusebius's summary of the charge against Paul of Samosata at the Council of Antioch (268); adoptionist Christology condemned as incompatible with orthodox teaching

text: We maintain that there is one God, but under this dispensation… which we call the economy, there is also a Son of this one God, his Word, who proceeded from him and through whom all things were made.
notes: Tertullian's counter to Marcion's two-god dualism: the Creator and Father of Jesus are one God

text: They maintain, then, that this Demiurge imagined that he created all these things of himself, while he in reality made them in conjunction with the productive power of Achamoth. He formed the heaven, yet was ignorant of the heavens; he fashioned man, yet knew not man.
notes: Irenaeus's summary of Valentinian Gnostic cosmology: the Demiurge (Creator) is ignorant of the higher spiritual realm

text: We receive both the Paraclete and the New Prophecy: from the Paraclete's gifts comes the composition of this work.
notes: Tertullian's declaration of Montanist allegiance in the preface to his most important theological work; demonstrates the overlap of Montanism and anti-Patripassianism in Tertullian

text: We baptize those who have come from heresy, because they did not receive a true baptism. For how could one who was not a Christian baptize into Christ?
work_reference: Homily on Luke 21 (Origen, in later catena traditions; cf. Cyprian's argument)
notes: Origen's position that heretical baptism is invalid; basis for Cyprian's rebaptism theology

text: We, indeed, reverently make use of the name of Jesus, recalling to memory his history. And it is not the graves of our holy men, but the souls of those who are buried there, which we honor as the souls of men devoted to the true God.
notes: Origen's qualified defence of Christian veneration of martyrs' graves against the pagan charge of tomb-worship

text: If anyone does not agree to the holy Mary bearing the title Theotokos, they are cut off from the divine nature. For it was not a mere man who was born of the Holy Virgin, but God incarnate.
notes: Origen is the earliest extant writer to use the title Theotokos (God-bearer) for Mary; the precise wording survives in later excerpts

text: Statues of him [Constantine] were set up in every province… and imperial laws were issued in all directions, setting forth the honor due to his memory.
notes: Eusebius records imperial image-veneration of Constantine; the Vita Constantini is among the earliest literary contexts for discussing the veneration of imperial images in a Christian setting, which shaped later debates about holy images
